question: **Elucidate the molecular mechanisms whereby tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy exerts its anti-neoplastic effects in gastrointestinal cancers, with specific reference to the TIL composition, antigen recognition, and the role of the tumor microenvironment (TME).**
answer:
TIL therapy exploits the endogenous cytotoxic potential of tumor-resident T lymphocytes, which are isolated from the neoplastic tissue, expanded ex vivo, and reinfused after lymphodepletion. At the cellular level, TILs comprise clonally expanded CD8⁺ effector memory T cells (CD8⁺CD45RO⁺CCR7⁻) and, to a lesser extent, CD4⁺ Th1 and Th17 subsets. These lymphocytes recognize tumor-associated antigens (TAAs) presented by HLA class I/II molecules via their αβ T-cell receptors (TCRs). Antigenic targets include shared self-antigens (e.g., carcinoembryonic antigen [CEA], MUC1), cancer-germline antigens (e.g., NY-ESO-1 encoded by CTAG1B), and mutated neoantigens derived from frameshift or missense mutations in driver genes such as KRAS, TP53, and APC.

The tumor microenvironment (TME) in gastrointestinal cancers (e.g., colorectal, gastric, pancreatic) is characterized by hypoxia, extracellular matrix (ECM) remodeling, metabolic immune suppression (via indoleamine-2,3-dioxygenase [IDO], arginase-1 [ARG1]), and immune checkpoint expression (PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3). Expansion of TILs in interleukin-2 (IL-2) and interleukin-15 (IL-15) cocktails, with or without TGF-β inhibition and metabolic reprogramming (e.g., via mTOR activation), enhances their polyfunctionality, persistence, and tumor homing. Upon re-infusion, TILs traffic to tumor deposits, where they engage in serial killing of MHC-matched neoplastic cells, release IFN-γ and TNF-α, and recruit innate effectors—natural killer (NK) cells and macrophages—via CXCL9/CXCL10 chemokine gradients.

Clinically, TIL therapy has demonstrated durable objective responses in 30–40 % of metastatic gastrointestinal cancer patients refractory to checkpoint blockade, particularly in mismatch-repair proficient colorectal cancer where checkpoint inhibitors fail. Recent phase II trials (NCT04426669, NCT03689889) report 5-year overall survival rates of 32 % post-TIL versus 15 % with standard-of-care chemotherapy, with therapy-related grade ≥3 adverse events (e.g., cytokine release syndrome [CRS]) in 25 % of patients. Current research focuses on TCR-engineered TILs against KRASG12D, IL-15 super-agonist-transduced TILs (NCT05477833), and combinations with CD40 agonists or focal adhesion kinase (FAK) inhibitors to disrupt the fibrotic stromal barrier. In summary, TIL therapy exemplifies precision immunotherapy by leveraging native tumor-reactive lymphocytes, overcoming TME immunosuppression, and delivering sustained clinical benefit in genomically complex gastrointestinal malignancies.
